question_text: Which statement best explains why the Southern Hemisphere has summer when the Northern Hemisphere has winter?

options.label: A
options.text: The Southern Hemisphere is closer to the Sun in December and January

options.label: B
options.text: It receives more direct rays because it's tilted towards the Sun during this time

options.label: C
options.text: The Southern Hemisphere spins faster on its axis during this period

options.label: D
options.text: The Sun is directly above the equator for six months of the year

correct_answer: It receives more direct rays because it's tilted towards the Sun during this time
explanation: Earth's axis is tilted at 23.5°. When the Southern Hemisphere is tilted towards the Sun, it receives more direct (perpendicular) rays, which are more intense and concentrated. This causes summer in the Southern Hemisphere, even though the distance to the Sun barely changes. The Northern Hemisphere is tilted away, receiving more oblique rays, causing winter.
